qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Root MCP OAuth endpoints expose internal-only OAuth2 servers and use stored client secrets
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding concerns unauthenticated root MCP OAuth endpoint resolution in `litellm/proxy/_experimental/mcp_server/discoverable_endpoints.py` and stored-client-secret token exchange, while the advisory describes password-hash disclosure from `/user/info`, `/user/update`, and `/spend/users` plus raw SHA-256 hash acceptance at `/v2/login`.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No final qualifying finding identifies the advisory's vulnerable password-handling chain. The only qualifying finding targets experimental MCP OAuth root endpoint filtering and stored-secret reuse, not password-hash exposure or `/v2/login` pass-the-hash authentication bypass.
evidence: The advisory describes three linked flaws: unsalted SHA-256 password storage, password-hash exposure from `/user/info`, `/user/update`, and `/spend/users`, and `/v2/login` accepting the raw SHA-256 hash as a valid password.

The only final `qualifying_vuln` finding is `mcp_root_oauth_private_server_exposure`, whose code locations are in `litellm/proxy/_experimental/mcp_server/discoverable_endpoints.py` and `mcp_server_manager.py` and whose exploit path is root MCP OAuth discovery/token routing.
The finding report and reproduced PoC do not mention the advisory endpoints, user password fields, SHA-256 password storage, scrypt migration, or login verification logic.
